meta.title: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool that walks employees through three psychological axes: Locus (Victim↔Victor), Orientation (Entitlement↔Contribution), and Radius (Self-centric↔Altrocentric).

nodes.text: Good evening. Set aside whatever's still open in another tab. This is just five minutes — for you to look at your day clearly, without judgment. Let's begin.
Without overthinking it — if today were a passenger in a car, which seat was it in?
options: Driving — I was steering things
Co-pilot — I contributed but wasn't in control
Back seat — things happened around me
Trunk — I was just along for the ride

match: Driving — I was steering things
Co-pilot — I contributed but wasn't in control
Back seat — things happened around me
Trunk — I was just along for the ride

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something worked out in your favour today, what made it happen?
options: I prepared or thought ahead
I adapted when the plan changed
I asked for what I needed
Honestly — I think I got lucky

match: I prepared or thought ahead
I adapted when the plan changed
I asked for what I needed
Honestly — I think I got lucky

nodes.text: There's a moment in most days where something could have gone either way. Did you have one of those today?
options: Yes — and I made a deliberate call
Yes — but I kind of stumbled into the right choice
Not sure — I was mostly reacting
No, today felt pretty predetermined

nodes.text: Luck is real — but it usually needs a door to walk through. Did you do anything today that made luck more likely?
options: I showed up consistently even when it didn't feel worth it
I kept options open instead of locking in too early
I can't really think of anything — it just happened
I was in the right place but I didn't put myself there

match: I showed up consistently even when it didn't feel worth it
I kept options open instead of locking in too early
I can't really think of anything — it just happened
I was in the right place but I didn't put myself there

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When things got difficult, what was your first instinct?
options: Look for what I could still influence
Wait for someone with more authority to step in
Push through alone, without asking for help
Feel stuck — it didn't feel like my call to make

match: Look for what I could still influence
Wait for someone with more authority to step in
Push through alone, without asking for help
Feel stuck — it didn't feel like my call to make

nodes.text: Looking back now — was there one small thing you could have changed, even if it wouldn't have fixed everything?
options: Yes — I can see it clearly in hindsight
Maybe — but I'm not sure it would have mattered
Possibly — I just didn't think of it in the moment
No — the constraints were genuinely outside my control

match: Yes — I can see it clearly in hindsight
Maybe — but I'm not sure it would have mattered
Possibly — I just didn't think of it in the moment
No — the constraints were genuinely outside my control

nodes.text: You came in as "{A1_OPEN.answer}" — and there's evidence to back that up. You made deliberate calls today, not just reactions. That's not arrogance; it's accountability in the good sense — you stayed in the conversation with your own day. Hold onto that.

Luck favours consistent effort. The fact that you can trace a thread between something you did and something that went well — that's not luck. That's cause and effect, quietly doing its job. Worth noticing.

Some days really are shaped by forces you didn't arrange. That's honest. The question for tomorrow is small: is there one thing — one door you could build — that makes good days slightly more likely? You don't need to answer that now.

Even in a day that felt like the back seat, you looked for what you could still steer. That's the move. Most people either surrender to the current or exhaust themselves fighting it — you did something in between. That's worth more than it sounds.

The fact that you can see the small thing in hindsight means the awareness is already there — it just arrived late. That's how it starts. Next time, it might arrive during the moment instead of after. That gap closes slowly, then quickly.

Sometimes the constraints really are the constraints. Accepting that honestly — not as an excuse, but as a clear-eyed reading of a situation — is its own kind of clarity. The question worth sitting with is: what's the smallest thing that *was* yours to move?
Okay. You've looked at how you handled what came at you today. Now let's look at something different — not how you reacted, but what you gave.
Think about the last hour of your working day. Which of these feels most true?
options: I was still thinking about what I hadn't received — recognition, clarity, resources
I was focused on finishing something that would help someone else
I helped someone without them having to ask
I was mostly thinking about whether I had done enough to be noticed

match: I was focused on finishing something that would help someone else
I helped someone without them having to ask
I was still thinking about what I hadn't received — recognition, clarity, resources
I was mostly thinking about whether I had done enough to be noticed

nodes.text: What's one specific thing you gave today — effort, attention, knowledge, presence — that wasn't strictly required of you?
options: Helped someone who was struggling, without being asked
Shared something I knew that made someone else's work easier
Did an unglamorous task because it needed doing
Stayed patient in a moment where impatience would have been easier

nodes.text: That feeling of not being seen — when did you first notice it today?
options: Early on, when something I did wasn't acknowledged
Mid-day, when someone else got credit for shared work
Late, when I compared my output to what I got back
It's been there all week, today just surfaced it

nodes.text: Here's a harder question: was there a moment today where someone else needed something from you — and you were too focused on your own score to fully show up for them?
options: Yes — and I know exactly the moment
Probably — I wasn't paying attention to notice
I don't think so, but I'm not certain
No — I held up my end

match: Yes — and I know exactly the moment
Probably — I wasn't paying attention to notice
I don't think so, but I'm not certain
No — I held up my end

nodes.text: The things you listed — none of them required a formal ask or a line in your job description. That's Organizational Citizenship: the tissue that holds teams together. It doesn't always get noticed. It gets *felt*. And it accumulates in ways that formal contributions don't.

You said "{A2_Q_ENTITLEMENT_FOLLOW.answer}". That moment you identified — it's not a verdict on who you are. It's data. The fact that you can name it means you're already past the first and hardest step: seeing it. Tomorrow, notice that moment earlier.

You're still carrying something from today — a sense that the ledger isn't balanced. That's human. But here's the thing about ledgers: the people you're measuring against usually aren't keeping the same count. One concrete act of giving — tomorrow, early — tends to reset the feeling faster than any recognition would.
Last section. You've looked at your agency and your giving. Now let's zoom out one more step — beyond you, beyond your immediate circle.
When you think about the most significant thing that happened today, who is in the frame?
options: Just me — it's about my performance, my goals
Me and one or two specific people I interact with directly
The wider team — we were all part of the same picture
Someone outside my immediate circle — a user, a client, or someone I don't usually think about

match: Just me — it's about my performance, my goals
Me and one or two specific people I interact with directly
The wider team — we were all part of the same picture
Someone outside my immediate circle — a user, a client, or someone I don't usually think about

nodes.text: That focus on yourself — was it a necessary narrowing today, or has it been the default setting for a while?
options: Necessary — I'm in a stretch that genuinely requires deep personal focus
Partly necessary, partly habit — I'm not always sure which
Mostly habit — I tend to process everything through my own lens first
Honestly, I hadn't noticed until this question

match: Necessary — I'm in a stretch that genuinely requires deep personal focus
Partly necessary, partly habit — I'm not always sure which
Mostly habit — I tend to process everything through my own lens first
Honestly, I hadn't noticed until this question

nodes.text: Is there someone — a colleague, a user, someone downstream of your work — who your day affected, but who you haven't thought about since the moment passed?
options: Yes — and thinking about them now actually shifts how I feel about today
Yes — but I'm not sure my day had much impact on them
Probably, but I genuinely don't have visibility into that
I'm drawing a blank right now

nodes.text: You're thinking about the people around you. Was there someone on your team today who had a harder day than you — and did you notice in the moment?
options: Yes — I noticed and I checked in or helped
Yes — I noticed but didn't act on it
I'm not sure — I was focused on my own workload
Not that I'm aware of, though I could have missed it

match: Yes — I noticed and I checked in or helped
Yes — I noticed but didn't act on it
I'm not sure — I was focused on my own workload
Not that I'm aware of, though I could have missed it

nodes.text: That's unusual — most people anchor to themselves first. What brought the wider perspective into focus today?
options: I received feedback from a user or someone outside the team
I saw the downstream effect of something I worked on
A conversation made me think past my immediate role
I've been trying to actively widen my perspective lately

nodes.text: Deep focus has its place — intense periods demand it. The thing to watch is the re-entry: when the sprint ends, does the wider view come back? The people around you probably adapted to your focused mode. They'll appreciate being seen again when you surface.

The fact that thinking about that person shifted something — even slightly — is important. That's not sentimentality. That's Maslow's self-transcendence: the realization that meaning expands when your concern does. You don't need to solve their problems. You just need to keep them in the frame.

You noticed someone's difficulty and moved toward it — not away from it. That's the radius widening in practice. It costs attention and sometimes time. But teams remember the person who showed up for them on a hard day long after they've forgotten the deliverable you hit.

It's easy to miss what's happening two desks over when your own day is full. Tomorrow, before you open your first task: one glance around. Not a formal check-in. Just a look. The people who feel seen are the ones who pull hardest when it matters.

You described today's most significant moment through someone else's experience. That's not nothing — most people never get there, or only arrive much later in their careers. The question now is whether you can make that perspective a habit rather than an occasional visitor.

Here's your day, reflected back:

You came in feeling like "{A1_OPEN.answer}" — and by the end of Axis 1, the picture was {axis1.summary}.

On contribution, {axis2.summary}.

And when it came to the wider picture, {axis3.summary}.

Three axes. One day. No verdict. Just a clearer view than you had five minutes ago.
That's it. Rest well. Tomorrow has its own shape — you'll show up with more to work with than you think.

axis1.dominant: computed: internal if internal >= external else external

summary.internal: the agency was there — even if imperfect, you were in it
summary.external: the day pulled harder than you did; the small choice you named is the thread to pull on tomorrow

axis2.dominant: computed: contribution if contribution >= entitlement else entitlement

summary.contribution: you gave more than was required — and that surplus is the good kind of debt the people around you carry
summary.entitlement: there's something worth recalibrating between what you gave and what you expected back

summary.self: your frame was close-range today — not wrong, but narrow
summary.other: your field of vision included the people around you — that's rarer than it sounds
summary.transcend: you looked past the immediate — past yourself, past your team — and found meaning in something larger
